coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-embedded fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystals hydrogel strip for accurate and selective detection of rutin in Sophora japonica products
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Xiaoqian
Pan, Mingfei
Hong, Liping
Liu, Kaixin
Yang, Jingying
Wang, Shan
Song, Yang
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: This study aimed to develop a fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystals hydrogel (FMIPH) strip based on carbon dots (CDs) and inverse opal photonic crystals (PCs) for the detection of the active ingredient rutin in Sophora japonica products. The FMIPH-based sensing system was constructed using rutin as template molecules, 4-vinylpyridine as functional monomers, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linker, and inverse opal PCs with three-dimensional macroporous structure as the skeleton. The prepared CDs, SiO2 microspheres, PCs model, and FMIPH were characterized in detail, and the prepared CDs-embedded FMIPH strip exhibited stable and sensitive reproducible fluorescence response, and selective recognition capability to rutin. The linear fluorescence response of CDs-embedded FMIPH to rutin was obtained in the concentration range of 2.5–40 μg mL−1 (R 2 = 0.9876) with a limit of detection of 2.3 μg mL−1 and a limit of quantitation of 7.2 μg mL−1. The CDs-embedded FMIPH strip obtained satisfying recoveries (81.7–95.6 %) with a relative standard deviation less than 3.1 % (n = 3) in spiked samples, having a good agreement with the results from high-performance liquid chromatography (R 2 = 0.9945). This portable strip could be used for rapid identification and quantitative detection of rutin in complex matrices, showing broad prospects in the quality evaluation and grading of functional foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rutin
CDs fluorescence
Inverse opal PCs
Molecularly imprinted photonic crystals hydrogel
Functional foods

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of monodisperse SiO2 microspheres and PCs model
Preparation of the rutin CDs-embedded FMIPH strip
Fluorescence response of CDs-embedded FMIPH/FNIPH strips to rutin
Fabrication of rutin CDs-embedded FMIPH strip
Surface morphology of SiO2, PCs model and FMIPH/FNIPH
Fluorescence response of CDs-embedded FMIPH to rutin
Kinetic response, selectivity, and stability of CDs-embedded FMIPH
Evaluation of CDs-embedded FMIPH application in rutin detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31972147 ), Project of Tianjin Science and Technology Plan (No. 20ZYJDJC00040 ), and Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology (No. SKLFNS-KF-202115 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation process of CDs-embedded FMIPH test strip.

(A) TEM image of CDs, inset: the corresponding diameter distribution. (B) Calibration curve of fluorescence intensity integration and ultraviolet (UV) absorbance of CDs and quinine sulfate (QS). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (D) UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs, inset: the images of CDs dispersion under natural and UV light (365 nm).
SEM images of SiO2 microspheres (A); PCs model (B); CDs-embedded FNIPH (C) and CDs-embedded FMIPH (D).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-embedded FNIPH (a) and FMIPH after (b) and before (c) elution. (B) UV absorption spectra of rutin (a) and CDs-embedded FMIPH (b). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs-embedded FMIPH upon the addition of rutin. (D) Calibration curve between the fluorescence response of CDs-embedded FMIPH and increasing rutin concentrations (2.5–40 μg mL−1) of three parallel experiments.

(A) Kinetic response of CDs-embedded FMIPH (a) and FNIPH (b) at different time in three parallel experiments. (B) Fluorescence response of CDs-embedded FMIPH and FNIPH strips to different analytes of three parallel experiments with the same concentration at 40 μg mL−1. (C) Fluorescence stability of CDs-embedded FMIPH strip within 10 days. (D) Fluorescence reproducibility of CDs-embedded FMIPH strip to rutin.
Application of the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip and HPLC method in spiked and recovery experiments of rutin in Sophora japonica products.".

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-embedded fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystals hydrogel strip for accurate and selective detection of rutin in Sophora japonica products

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Animal and Plant Resistance, College of Life Sciences, Tianjin Normal University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Animal and Plant Resistance, College of Life Sciences, Tianjin Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Animal and Plant Resistance, College of Life Sciences, Tianjin Normal University, Tianjin 300387, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aimed to develop a fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystals hydrogel (FMIPH) strip based on carbon dots (CDs) and inverse opal photonic crystals (PCs) for the detection of the active ingredient rutin in Sophora japonica products. The FMIPH-based sensing system was constructed using rutin as template molecules, 4-vinylpyridine as functional monomers, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linker, and inverse opal PCs with three-dimensional macroporous structure as the skeleton. The prepared CDs, SiO2 microspheres, PCs model, and FMIPH were characterized in detail, and the prepared CDs-embedded FMIPH strip exhibited stable and sensitive reproducible fluorescence response, and selective recognition capability to rutin. The linear fluorescence response of CDs-embedded FMIPH to rutin was obtained in the concentration range of 2.5–40 μg mL−1 (R 2 = 0.9876) with a limit of detection of 2.3 μg mL−1 and a limit of quantitation of 7.2 μg mL−1. The CDs-embedded FMIPH strip obtained satisfying recoveries (81.7–95.6 %) with a relative standard deviation less than 3.1 % (n = 3) in spiked samples, having a good agreement with the results from high-performance liquid chromatography (R 2 = 0.9945). This portable strip could be used for rapid identification and quantitative detection of rutin in complex matrices, showing broad prospects in the quality evaluation and grading of functional foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Rutin is an active ingredient in Flos Sophorae Immaturus (a “medicinal and food homologous” food), which is considered as a flavonoid derivative with a wide range of sources. Rutin flavonoids have been widely used in clinical treatment, dietary regulation or the development of functional foods due to their free-radical scavenging, immunomodulatory, anti-oxidation, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial and other functions [1,2]. In the field of food and medicine, Flos Sophorae Immaturus is the main source of rutin flavonoids. It is even an important functional food of import and export standards [3,4]. The content of rutin in Flos Sophorae Immaturus is closely related to varieties, producing areas and environmental conditions, which can be used as a reference standard to evaluate the quality of Sophora japonica and its products. Therefore, the quantitative analysis of the iconic active ingredient rutin is helpful to the quality classification and evaluation of functional foods such as Sophorae japonica products, which is more conducive to the formulation and revision of relevant import and export standards.

Currently, the strategies for determining the content of rutin flavonoids are mainly based on large-scale analytical instruments, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5,6] and high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [7,8], capillary electrophoresis [9,10] and electrochemical method [11,12]. Although these methods are efficient, accurate and can almost cover the detection of most ingredients in foods, the shortcomings of expensive analytical instruments, relatively cumbersome testing process, and time-consuming process restrict their application in rapid screening, and even real-time online detection. Fluorescence sensing has the characteristics of high sensitivity, fast speed, and easy operation, having been one of the research hotspots and applied in various detection fields [13–15]. At present, the fluorescence signal for the analysis mainly comes from organic fluorescent dyes, metal nanoclusters, carbon dots (CDs), and other nanoscale materials [16–18]. These materials could be further applied to the development of miniaturized and integrated fluorescence sensing and detection devices. The CDs have excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, and can be combined with other materials to prepare nanocomposites, which have been widely used in the study of fluorescence sensing [19,20].

Identification of trace targets accurately and effectively in complex matrices is necessary to analyze ingredients in food samples. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are a kind of artificially synthesized biomimetic host-guest complex that can specifically recognize targets, which have been widely used in the purification of food matrices and the enrichment and identification of trace targets [21–23]. The focus of current research is to maintain the specific recognition ability of MIPs for the targets, while solving the problem of mass transfer barriers in the process of molecular recognition, which is vital in improving the detection efficiency of food. The inverse opal photonic crystals (PCs) have a three-dimensional macroporous structure that can provide more mass transfer channels for the molecularly imprinted polymerization system, which is more suitable for improving mass transfer efficiency than its Bragg diffraction performance [24–26]. This is beneficial to the rapid diffusion of the targets, thereby increasing the mass transfer speed in the highly crosslinked system. Fluorescent molecularly imprinted materials using inverse opal PCs as the skeleton not only have high porosity, but also can form specific recognition sites in the imprinting system [27,28]. This can increase the number of specific recognition sites and their spatial arrangement order to a certain extent, which is beneficial to improve the selective recognition ability of fluorescent imprinted materials.

In this study, a CDs-embedded fluorescent molecularly imprinted photonic crystal hydrogel (FMIPH) with a specific recognition ability for rutin was prepared using inverse opal PCs as the skeleton. A portable strip for rapid fluorescence detection of rutin was further developed. The fabricated CDs-embedded FMIPH strip exhibited good fluorescence response and selective recognition ability to rutin, providing an effective and promising strategy for the quality evaluation and grading of functional foods.
See supplementary material SI. 1 for details.

The fluorescent CDs were synthesized based on a previous report with slight modifications [29]. Briefly, citric acid (0.46 g, 2.39 mmol) and o-phenylenediamine (0.21 g, 1.94 mmol) were accurately weighed and dissolved in ultrapure water (10.0 mL), and further transferred into one Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and hydrothermally heated at 200 ℃ for 5 h. After centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, the resulting supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 µm nylon filter and dialyzed using a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 24 h in ultrapure water. The obtained CDs dispersion liquid (about 8.0 mL) was stored at 4 ℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of monodisperse SiO2 microspheres and PCs model
ce:section.ce:para: Solution A containing ethanol (40.0 mL), NH3·H2O (7.7 mL), and ultrapure water (2.23 mL) were prepared in a 250 mL glass flask treated using a chromic acid solution. Then, solution B containing ethanol (50.0 mL) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 2.29 mL) was quickly added to solution A. After magnetic stirring at room temperature for 12 h, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with ethanol three times. The remaining precipitation was dried at 50 °C for 12 h to obtain a white powder (SiO2 microspheres).

The glassy slides were immersed in a mixture of 98.0 % H2SO4 and 30.0 % H2O2 (7: 3, v/v) for 24 h, then rinsed with ultrapure water, ultrasonically washed in ethanol for 15 min, and finally dried by nitrogen (N2) flow for use. A monodisperse SiO2 microsphere suspension (1.0 %, w/v) containing SiO2 powder (50.0 mg) and ethanol (6.337 mL) was prepared in a beaker for the preparation of the PCs model. The hydrophilic glass slides were vertically put into the suspension to allow the self-assembly of SiO2 colloidal particles for 12 h at 50 ℃. After the ethanol was completely volatilized, the PCs model was formed on the surface of the glass slide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the rutin CDs-embedded FMIPH strip
ce:section.ce:para: Template rutin (61.05 mg, 0.1 mmol) was accurately weighed and dissolved in 1.0 mL of methanol. Fluorescent CDs dispersion liquid (250 μL) and functional monomer 4-vinylpyridine (4-VP, 42.92 μL, 0.4 mmol) were added. After ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, the mixture was stored in the dark at 4 ℃. The cross-linker ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, 75.44 μL, 0.4 mmol) and initiator azobisisobutyronitrile (15 mg) were subsequently added to the mixture. After ultrasonic treatment and N2 deoxygenation for 10 min, the resulting rutin molecularly imprinted pre-polymerization solution was sealed and stored in the dark at 4 ℃. A non-molecularly imprinted pre-polymerization solution was also prepared following the aforementioned procedure but in the absence of template rutin.

A piece of plexiglass PMMA was covered on the surface of the glass slide containing PCs to form a “sandwich” structure by clamping the two ends with dovetail clips. The fluorescent molecularly imprinted or non-molecularly imprinted pre-polymerization solution was slowly injected into the gap of the “sandwich” structure through capillary force. Under the protection of N2, the “sandwich” structure filled with the pre-polymerization solution was placed at 60 ℃ for 4 h for thermal polymerization in the dark. Then, 5 % hydrofluoric acid (HF) solution was applied to soak the “sandwich” structure to remove the glass slide and the SiO2 template, and the polymer film with an inverse opal structure was left on the surface of the PMMA slide. The template rutin was removed by immersing in the mixture of methanol/acetic acid (18: 1, v/v) for 3 h (the eluent was changed every hour). Subsequently, the obtained CDs-embedded FMIPH and fluorescent non-molecularly imprinted photonic crystals hydrogel (FNIPH) on the PMMA slide surface were fully washed with methanol and ultrapure water, and stored in ultrapure water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs-embedded FMIPH/FNIPH strips to rutin
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the prepared CDs-embedded FMIPH and FNIPH strips to rutin and the analogs was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The prepared CDs-embedded FMIPH or FNIPH strips were immersed in 5.0 mL of methanol containing the analyte at different concentrations of 2.5–40 μg mL−1 for 20 min at room temperature in the dark. The fluorescence performance of the CDs-embedded FMIPH and FNIPH strips was investigated under the following conditions: FMIPH/FNIPH strips were placed in the sample pool at a 45° angle; the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, the excitation and emission slit width was 5 nm, the photomultiplier tube voltage was 600 V, and the scanning range was 400–600 nm.

Commercial functional foods such as Flos Sophorae Immaturus and Flos Sophorae, rich in rutin flavonoids were selected to evaluate the applicability of the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip in the fluorescence analysis of rutin content. The dried samples were crushed using a pulverizer and sieved through a 100-mesh sieve. A total of 100.0 mg of the sieved sample powder was soaked using 50.0 mL of methanol for 1 h, and the mixture was then ultrasonically extracted at 55 ℃ for 40 min. After cooling to the room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged, and the resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon filter and diluted to 50 mL with methanol in a volumetric flask. Then, 2.5 mL of the supernatant was diluted 20 times again with methanol, and the resulting solution was used for the measurement of rutin content using the CDs-embedded FMIPH strip and HPLC method. In the spiked and recovery experiments, the sieved samples of Flos Sophorae Immaturus and Flos Sophorae were first spiked at three rutin levels of 5.0, 10.0, and 20.0 μg mL−1 and further treated according to the aforementioned procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of rutin CDs-embedded FMIPH strip
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs, SiO2 microspheres, and rutin molecularly imprinted pre-polymerization solution were prepared to develop a CDs-embedded FMIPH strip in this study. Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of the preparation process of CDs-embedded FMIPH strip. Suitable SiO2 microspheres and fluorescent CDs were prepared by weak alkaline hydrolysis and hydrothermal methods, respectively. One closely arranged PCs film was formed on a glass slide by the vertical deposition self-assembly of SiO2 microspheres. Furthermore, a molecularly imprinted pre-polymerization solution containing rutin, CDs, 4-VP, and EGDMA was added to the gaps of the “sandwich” structure containing the PCs film, thereby forming the molecularly imprinted crosslinked system embedded with CDs by thermal initiation. Importantly, the SiO2 microspheres were further removed to form a three-dimensional inverse opal structure, and the template rutin was removed to form cavities or sites with specific recognition ability for rutin. When rutin was combined again, the fluorescence of CDs embedded in the three-dimensional molecular imprinting system could be quenched, and the quantitative analysis and rapid detection of rutin could be realized based on the change in the fluorescence signal.

In this study, the CDs were embedded in a molecularly imprinted crosslinked system to provide the fluorescence signal of the FMIPH strip. The fluorescence performance of CDs could affect the accuracy and sensitivity of the FMIPH strip for rutin detection.

As shown in Fig. S1, the FT-IR spectrum was used for the structural identification of CDs. The broad peak at 3425 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H. The absorption peaks at 2920 cm−1, 1717 cm−1, and 1221 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of C-H, CO, and C-O, respectively. The typical bending vibration of C-H and C-N appeared at 1386 cm−1. These results indicated the existence of hydrophilic groups, such as -OH, -COOH, and -NH2 on the surface of the prepared CDs. Fig. S2 presents the Raman spectrum of the prepared CDs. The weaker peak at 1578 cm−1 was caused by the G-band sp2 hybridization while no obvious characteristic peak was observed at 1360 cm−1, indicating the synthesized CDs had an amorphous structure. The prepared CDs had an average particle size of 8 nm ( Fig. 2 A) and a high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 40.15% calculated using QS (QY: 54 %) as the reference (Fig. 2B, SI. 2). Under different excitation wavelengths (320–400 nm), the emission wavelength of CDs basically remained unchanged, indicating that the photoluminescence behavior of CDs was independent of the excitation wavelength, laying a foundation for rutin detection (Fig. 2C). Fig. 2D shows the UV and fluorescence spectra of CDs, with a maximum emission wavelength of 465 nm. A slight gap was found between the maximum excitation wavelength (370 nm) and the UV absorption wavelength (365 nm), which was attributed to the nonradiative relaxation of CDs at high energy levels [30,31]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs stored at different environment changes with relative standard deviation (RSD) < 4.0 %, (n = 6, stability: 3.1 %, photobleaching resistance: 4.0 %, salt tolerance: 2.8 %), which was considered to be within the acceptable range (Fig. S3). These results indicated that the prepared CDs had suitable structural features and remarkable fluorescence properties and could easily penetrate the inverse opal PCs gaps and embed them into FMIPH strips, offering the fluorescence signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology of SiO2, PCs model and FMIPH/FNIPH
ce:section.ce:para: Monodisperse SiO2 microspheres were prepared by the hydrolysis of TEOS in a weakly alkaline environment. Through the processes of vertical deposition and self-assembly, and evaporation of ethanol in a controlled temperature gradient environment, a densely arranged SiO2-based PCs model with a face-centered cubic structure was formed on the glass slides [32].

Fig. 3 A and 3B show the surface morphology of SiO2 microspheres synthesized by the Stöber method and the obtained PCs model by the colloidal template method, respectively. The prepared SiO2 microspheres were observed to be uniform with an average size of 300 nm, and the obtained PCs model were closely arranged and exhibited a typical face-centered cubic structure. The volume of SiO2 microspheres shrinks due to the instability of environmental factors during the preparation process, resulting in cracks and dislocations on the deposition layer of the PCs model [33]. After dissolving SiO2 microspheres with HF, the three-dimensional porous structure formed on the prepared PCs model increased the internal orientation of the binding of template molecules and functional monomers, contributing to the formation of specific recognition sites in the process of molecular imprinting. As the preparation of FNIPH does not involve the participation of the rutin template, only a nonspecific polymer film with crosslinked disorder and broken pore structure can be formed after removing the SiO2 template. Compared with the surface morphological characterization of a disordered and cracked polymer film of FNIPH (Fig. 3C), FMIPH formed a three-dimensional porous and ordered inverse opal structure after removing SiO2 microspheres and template rutin (Fig. 3D), which would play an important role in the specific recognition of rutin molecules.

In this study, the dosage of CDs to be added, the amount and proportion of template molecules, functional monomers, and cross-linker were examined in detail to obtain the CDs-embedded FMIPH strip with good mass transfer ability, excellent selective recognition performance and sensitive fluorescence response. The dosage of CDs added was directly related to the fluorescence performance of the prepared CDs-embedded FMIPH, which affected the sensitivity of the detection. Table S1 shows the fluorescence response (F 0/F) and imprinting factor (IF) results of FMIPH prepared using different CDs dosages. As the dosage of CDs added increased, the F 0/F and IF value of FMIPH changed significantly and reached the maximum (1.178 and 3.708) at 250 μL of CDs dosage. Adding more CDs could have easily enhanced nonspecific fluorescence. Therefore, the dosage of CDs used in this study was set at 250 μL. In the imprinting process, the molar ratio between rutin and functional monomer (4-VP) determines the selectivity and sensitivity of FMIPH recognition to rutin. The amount of cross-linker EGDMA is important to maintain the stability of the inverse opal PCs skeleton, the integrity of the three-dimensional ordered macroporous structure and the flexibility of FMIPH [34,35]. As shown in Fig. S4A and S4B, when the molar ratio of rutin to 4-VP was 1:4, and the molar ratio of 4-VP to EGDMA was 1:1, both F 0/F and IF values of CDs-embedded FMIPH reached the maximum. Under conditions of the optimum molar ratio, one CDs-embedded FMIPH equipped with stable and flexible three-dimensional macroporous structure and enough fast-binding sites was formed, thereby promoting the rapid elution and rebinding of template molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs-embedded FMIPH to rutin
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence characteristics of the prepared CDs-embedded FMIPH and FNIPH were compared and evaluated ( Fig. 4A). The CD-embedded FMIPH showed weak fluorescence before the removal of rutin due to the quenching effect of rutin on the fluorescence of CDs. After removing the rutin template, the fluorescence intensity increased significantly at the same excitation wavelength (λ = 370 nm). This result indicated that the rutin template was effectively removed from CDs-embedded FMIPH, forming the matching cavities or binding sites. At the same time, due to the random distribution of rutin and CDs in the system, the fluorescence of some CDs was not quenched by rutin. When the imprinted polymerization network structure was formed, the unquenched CDs were embedded in the interior, resulting in the higher fluorescence intensity of the FMIPH strip before elution. Based on this, the quenching mechanism of rutin on CDs-embedded FMIPH was further speculated. No overlap occurred between the UV absorption peak of rutin (200–400 nm) and the fluorescence emission peak of the FMIPH (440–480 nm), so the possibility of energy transfer was ruled out. Fig. 4B shows the UV absorption spectra of rutin and CDs-embedded FMIPH material. A similar absorption band gap could be clearly observed, which allowed charge transfer from the conduction band of CDs-embedded FMIPH to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of rutin, resulting in fluorescence quenching [36,37].

The fluorescence response of the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip to the target rutin was investigated at various concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-embedded FMIPH strip gradually decreased with the increase in the rutin concentration. This is because the rutin molecules were specifically recognized and bound by the FMIPH imprinting layer, resulting in fluorescence quenching of CDs. Fig. 4D shows the linear relationship between F 0/F of the CDs-embedded FMIPH strip and the concentrations of rutin (2.5–40 μg mL−1), which conforms to the Stern-Volmer equation
F
0
/ F =
K
SV
C
rutin
+ 1
, in which F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of rutin, respectively; K sv is the quenching constant; and C rutin is the rutin concentration. The limit of detection (S/N = 3) was calculated to be 2.3 μg mL−1, and the limit of quantitation was 7.2 μg mL−1 (S/N = 10). These results demonstrated that the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip had a good fluorescence response to the analyte rutin and provided a sensitive and effective strategy for rutin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic response, selectivity, and stability of CDs-embedded FMIPH
ce:section.ce:para: Low mass transfer resistance in the molecular imprinting system is conducive to the rapid diffusion of the target analyte to the specific recognition sites, thus improving the detection efficiency. In this study, CDs-embedded FMIPH with a three-dimensional ordered structure was prepared, and its response speed to external rutin stimulation was further evaluated. The prepared CDs-embedded FMIPH and FNIPH strips were immersed in a rutin standard solution (5.0 mL, 40 μg mL−1) for different time (1–30 min), and the fluorescence intensity of FMIPH and FNIPH were measured at room temperature. The kinetic response results of CDs-embedded FMIPH and FNIPH on rutin are shown in Fig. 5A. Obviously, CDs-embedded FMIPH had a higher fluorescence response to rutin than FNIPH during the same time. When exposed to rutin solution for 20 min, the F 0/F value reached the maximum and tended to be stable, indicating that the adsorption of rutin by CDs-embedded FMIPH reached saturation. An adsorption time of 20 min was enough to achieve adsorption diffusion equilibrium for rutin in the fabricated CDs-embedded FMIPH sensing system, which was selected in further study.

The selectivity of the CDs-embedded FMIPH strip mainly depended on the specific recognition of rutin by the molecularly imprinted layer. In this study, the analogs including quercetin (Qut), kaempferol (Kae), isorhamnetin (Iso) and chlorogenic acid (Cga), which may interfere with the detection of rutin, were selected to evaluate the selective recognition ability of CDs-embedded FMIPH strip for rutin at 40 μg mL−1. By comparing the fluorescence response with CDs-embedded FNIPH strip, the imprinting characteristics of the FMIPH imprinted layer were evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, CDs-embedded FMIPH was found to bind more rutin molecules than their corresponding FNIPH and showed higher selectivity toward rutin than other analogs and the IF for rutin in the FMIPH imprinted layer was calculated to be 6.65. The reason is that rutin was added into the pre-polymerization solution of FMIPH as a template molecule, so more recognition sites or cavities matching rutin were formed in the molecularly imprinted system. Meanwhile, the anti-interference ability of the developed FMIPH was investigated by adding equal amounts of structural analogs into the rutin solution to constitute the mixed system (40 μg mL−1). The fluorescence response of the FMIPH in the mixed system was higher than that of every single analog, and showed no significant changes compared with the rutin response alone, indicating the specific recognition ability for rutin and the anti-interference ability for the analogs (Fig. S5). This ability is primarily due to the removal of the template molecule rutin from the polymerization system with the three-dimensional ordered network structure provided by inverse opal PCs during the imprinting process, and the resulting binding sites played a key role in rebinding the rutin molecule. Importantly, this synergistic selectivity from MIPs and CDs and the high mass transfer efficiency of inverse opal PCs makes the FMIPH strip possess high recognition selectivity and accuracy toward rutin.

The fluorescence stability is a critical characteristic of the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH, which affects the application capability of FMIPH-based strips in rutin detection. The fluorescence intensity of one CDs-embedded FMIPH strip was measured within 10 days. As shown in Fig. 5 C, the value of fluorescence intensity of one FMIPH strip hardly decreased with a lower RSD of 4.5 % (n = 10), even after 10 days of storage. For different FMIPH strips equipped with stable fluorescence response to rutin, Fig. S6 shows the fabrication reproducibility of the FMIPH strip with an RSD of 1.2 %. The synthesized CDs-embedded FMIPH strip had not only highly crosslinked properties but also excellent flexible structure of inverse opal PCs. It could be easily eluted previously bound template molecules, thereby restoring the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-embedded FMIPH strip. Fig. 5D illustrates the change in fluorescence intensity obtained from five adsorption-elution cycles. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-embedded FMIPH strip still retained 92.8 %. The inevitable decrease in the fluorescence signal of the FMIPH imprinted system may be due to the loss of CDs, the disruption of the recognition site of rutin, or the collapse of the FMIPH strip structure due to multiple elutions. These results demonstrated the good fluorescence stability, reproducibility, and photobleaching resistance of the CDs-embedded FMIPH strip, laying the foundation for the subsequent application of this material in the detection of rutin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of CDs-embedded FMIPH application in rutin detection
ce:section.ce:para: Sophora japonica products including Flos Sophorae Immaturus and Flos Sophorae were selected and spiked at three levels of rutin (5.0, 10.0, and 20.0 μg mL−1) and analyzed using the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip to evaluate the performance of the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH test strip applied in real samples. Each level spiked in each sample was determined at least three times and the average content of rutin and the RSD were calculated.

Table 1 shows the recoveries of rutin in selected samples ranging from 81.7 % to 95.6 % with RSDs (3.1 %, n = 3) using the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip, which was in good agreement with the results of HPLC (R 2 = 0.9945) (Fig. S7). This confirmed the reliability and accuracy of the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip in the quantitative determination of rutin in complex food samples. Table S2 illustrates the comparison of different strategies for rutin detection, further demonstrating the merits of the proposed FMIPH strip in sample analysis, linear range, sensitivity and time consumption. These results indicated that the developed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip provided a promising strategy for the quantitative detection of rutin in functional food samples, which could be applied in the quality evaluation and grading of functional foods.

In summary, an efficient CDs-embedded FMIPH strip for the detection of rutin in Sophora japonica products was developed by combining CDs fluorescence sensing, inverse opal PCs, and molecular imprinting in the current study. This study was novel in detecting rutin using molecular imprinting fluorescence sensing on the inverse opal PCs skeleton with a three-dimensional macroporous structure. The proposed CDs-embedded FMIPH strip exhibited a fast response speed and remarkable fluorescence reproducibility, also high accuracy and specificity to rutin, which offered an efficient and promising strategy for rapid screening and grading of Sophora japonica products. In addition, this portable FMIPH strip was easy to prepare, store and carry, and could be used in combination with intelligent testing equipment, showing a wide range of applications for on-site, real-time or remote monitoring of the quality of functional foods.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31972147), Project of Tianjin Science and Technology Plan (No. 20ZYJDJC00040), and Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology (No. SKLFNS-KF-202115).

Xiaoqian Xie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - Original Draft. Mingfei Pan: Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Funding acquisition, Liping Hong: Data Curation, Investigation, Kaixin Liu: Formal analysis, Supervision, Jingying Yang: Writing - Review & Editing, Shan Wang: Software, Validation, Yang Song: Writing - Review & Editing, Shuo Wang: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the timely help given by Dr. Dongsheng Zheng and his group in improving the language of the article.

B-Chem. 252 (2017) 934–943.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.L. Zu, F.Y. Yan, Z.J. Bai, J.X. Xu, Y.Y. Wang, Y.C. Huang, X.G. Zhou, The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: a review on mechanisms and applications, Microchim. Acta 184 (2017) 1899–1914.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaoqian Xie is currently a master's student majoring in Food Science, State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. She has been working on developing novel nanomaterials-based fluorescence sensors for food analysis. She has published several academic papers in well-known journals such as Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry, Nanomaterials.

Mingfei Pan is serving as an associate professor in State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. His research mainly focuses on the development of novel biomimetic or biosensing devices based on various nanomaterials including their applications in food analysis and environment monitoring. He has published a few widely cited research papers in international core journals such as Biosensors and Bioelectronics, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, Foods etc.

Liping Hong is currently a master's student majoring in Food and Nutrition at Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her main research direction is to develop highly sensitive biomimetic sensing detection methods for various contaminants in foods.

Kaixin Liu is currently a master's student majoring in Food Science, State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her main research direction is developing new nanomaterials-based substrates for electrochemical biomimetic sensor and further applied them in food hazardous substances monitoring.

Jingying Yang is currently a master's student majoring in Food Science, State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her research mainly focuses on the development of novel nano SERS probe and substrate and applied them in the analysis of food and environmental contaminants.

Shan Wang is currently a master's student in State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her research mainly focuses on the development of nanomaterials-based chemical sensors and their application in food analysis.

Yang Song is serving as an associate professor in Tianjin Key Laboratory of Animal and Plant Resistance, Tianjin Normal University. Her research mainly focuses on the development of novel biomimetic or biosensing devices and their applications for the detection of food safety.

Shuo Wang received his Ph.D. in form The University of Sydney, now he is serving as a specially appointed professor/doctoral supervisor, in State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. He has long been engaged in the research fields of Food Safety and Food Science and elected as the academic Fellow of International Academy of Food Science and Technology (IAFoST) in 2017.
